planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Minneola tangelo requires cross-pollination from a compatible citrus (Temple orange, Dancy tangerine, or Orlando tangelo) for reliable, heavy fruit set — plant at least one pollinizer tree within 50 feet.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Minneola tangelo is a hybrid of Duncan grapefruit and Dancy tangerine developed by USDA breeders at the University of Florida in 1931, producing the largest and most dramatically flavored tangelo available — large (3–4 inch diameter), deep reddish-orange fruits with a characteristic knobby protrusion at the stem end (giving them the nickname 'Honeybell' from their bell-like silhouette) and intensely aromatic, sweet-tart juice that is considered by many citrus enthusiasts to be among the finest citrus flavors of any variety. Minneola typically ripens January–March in Florida and California (one of the latest-ripening citrus), and the fruits hold on the tree for 2–4 weeks allowing flexible harvest. The skins peel easily (a peel-and-eat citrus), and the flesh separates into 10–12 clean segments with very few seeds. The tree is medium-sized (8–15 feet), moderately vigorous, and somewhat cold-hardy for a tangelo. Minneola requires cross-pollination — plant a Temple orange, Dancy tangerine, or Orlando tangelo within 50 feet for adequate fruit set; trees planted alone may produce poorly. Outstanding for Gulf Coast gardens in zone 9–11.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center
University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

scientific_name: Citrus grandis x C. paradisi 'Oro Blanco'

planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Oro Blanco is self-fruitful and sets good crops without a cross-pollinator, though bee activity always improves fruit set.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Oro Blanco ('White Gold' in Spanish) is a remarkable hybrid of pomelo (Citrus grandis) and white grapefruit (Citrus paradisi) developed by University of California breeders in the 1950s and released in 1980, producing pale greenish-yellow to yellow fruits that appear externally unripe but are actually fully ripe and extraordinarily sweet — completely lacking the bitter notes of standard grapefruit due to the absence of the flavanone naringin, the compound responsible for grapefruit's characteristic bitterness. The result is a large citrus (slightly larger than grapefruit) with thick, spongy peel, white, juicy flesh, and a flavor that is pure sweet citrus without any bitterness — outstanding for people who love grapefruit flavor but dislike the bitterness, and for those who take medications contraindicated with grapefruit. Fruits ripen October through February (one of the earliest-ripening grapefruits) and hold on the tree for months; the thick peel insulates the flesh and extends storage life. The large tree (12–18 feet) is handsome in the landscape and produces heavily. A superb and under-known citrus for Gulf South zone 9 gardens.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection
University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center

common_name: Marsh Grapefruit (Seedless White Grapefruit, Marsh Seedless)

planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; grapefruit trees are large and require adequate space (20–25 feet diameter at maturity), so site carefully; Marsh is self-fruitful and productive without a pollinator.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Marsh grapefruit (introduced commercially around 1860 from a seedling found in Florida) is the foundational white grapefruit of the citrus industry — virtually all seedless white grapefruits sold commercially today are Marsh or descended from it. The large, round, pale-yellow fruits (4–5 inches diameter) are nearly or completely seedless (a major practical advantage over older seedy varieties like Duncan), with pale-cream to light-yellow flesh, balanced sweet-tart grapefruit flavor, and abundant juice. The flavor is the benchmark classic grapefruit flavor — moderately tart with characteristic bitterness from naringin — excellent eaten fresh (halved and sectioned with a serrated spoon), juiced, or used in salads and cocktails. The large tree (15–20 feet when mature, larger on vigorous rootstocks) is cold-hardier than many citrus and can tolerate brief temperatures to about 26–28°F when established. Marsh grapefruit is the parent of most modern grapefruit varieties, including Ruby Red (a bud sport of Marsh) and Thompson Pink. Ripens November through May in Florida; the fruits hold on the tree exceptionally well, improving in flavor through winter.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center
University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection

common_name: Ponkan Mandarin (Chinese Honey Orange, Taiwanese Mandarin)

planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun; Ponkan is self-fruitful and typically produces good crops without a pollinator, though it is alternate-bearing (heavy crop one year, lighter the next) and benefits from fruit thinning in heavy crop years to prevent limb breakage and encourage more even annual bearing.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Ponkan (also called 'Chinese Honey Orange', 'Warnurco', or 'Batangas' in the Philippines) is one of the most widely grown mandarin varieties in the world, dominant in China, Taiwan, Brazil, Japan, and throughout Southeast Asia — though less well known in North America than Satsuma or Clementine types. It produces large (3–4 inch diameter), round to slightly flattened, loose-skinned fruits (the skin slips off in large intact pieces much more easily than tighter-skinned mandarins) with exceptionally sweet, very low-acid juice and deeply orange, highly fragrant flesh divided into 10–13 segments with typically few seeds. The flavor profile emphasizes sweetness and intense mandarin aroma more than tartness, making Ponkan distinctively different from the more acid Clementine and Satsuma — excellent for people who prefer very sweet citrus. Ripens November through January in subtropical climates. The tree is medium-large and productive under good subtropical conditions in zone 9–11; it is moderately cold-hardy (tolerates brief temperatures to 26–28°F when established). An outstanding landscape and edible tree for Gulf South gardens.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection
University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center

planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Fairchild tangerine requires cross-pollination from a compatible citrus variety (such as Dancy tangerine, Orlando tangelo, or other mandarins) for adequate fruit set — plant a pollinizer within 50 feet.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Fairchild tangerine is a Clementine x Orlando tangelo hybrid developed by USDA breeders in 1964, producing deep reddish-orange, medium-sized (2.5–3 inch) fruits with rich, sweet-tart, spicy-aromatic tangerine flavor — one of the most intensely flavored mandarins available. The skin is thin, slightly rough, and adheres somewhat to the flesh (tighter than most loose-skinned mandarins but peels relatively easily by hand); fruits typically contain 6–12 seeds, which is a trade-off for the exceptional flavor. Fairchild is one of the earlier-ripening mandarins in the California/Gulf South season, ripening November through December, and is an excellent choice for gardeners who want full-size mandarin flavor earlier in the season than the late-ripening Honey or Murcott types. The tree is medium-sized (8–14 feet), well-branched, and productive under good conditions with a cross-pollinator present. Trees in zone 9 should be sited in the warmest available microclimate as Fairchild is somewhat cold-sensitive; it tolerates brief temperatures to about 26–28°F when established but young trees need frost protection in the first 2–3 years.
sources: University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection
University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center

planting.note: Plant container-grown, grafted trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; mandarins are generally more cold-hardy than sweet oranges and many are self-fruitful, though cross-pollination from other citrus usually improves fruit set and fruit size.

directions: Plant grafted trees in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Citrus reticulata is the species encompassing all mandarins, tangerines, satsumas, clementines, and most mandarin-type hybrids — a diverse group of loose-skinned, easily peeled, aromatic citrus originating in Southeast Asia and southern China that includes hundreds of named varieties ranging from tiny, seedless clementines to large, deeply flavored Ponkans. As a group, mandarins are the most cold-hardy common citrus (Satsuma types tolerating temperatures to 18–22°F when established) and one of the most adaptable to Gulf South conditions, performing well in zones 8b–11 where sweet oranges or grapefruit might struggle with cold or humidity-related issues. Key mandarin varieties for Gulf South gardens include Satsuma (most cold-hardy), Owari Satsuma, Owens Navel, Clementine, Ponkan, Murcott (Honey mandarin), and Dancy tangerine. Most mandarins ripen November through February, and the loose skin that peels easily by hand makes them the most user-friendly fresh citrus. The species is very adaptable to container culture, making it practical for zone 8 gardeners who want moveable citrus.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Citrus Research and Education Center
University of California Riverside Citrus Variety Collection
